perturbation.original_text: Invoices will be paid within 15 days of receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Invoices will be paid within 45 days of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term to 45 days might conflict with California Prompt Payment laws if these services are considered construction-related, which have stricter timelines.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159 sets requirements for home improvement contracts, including payment schedules. Although this contract doesn't explicitly state it's for home improvement, if the services provided fall under this category, a 45-day payment term could violate regulations on how payments can be structured and demanded.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159. (a) (1) This section identifies the projects for which a home improvement contract is required, outlines the contract requirements, and lists the items that shall be included in the contract, or may be provided as an attachment. (2) This section does not apply to service and repair contracts that are subject to Section 7159.10, if the contract for the applicable services complies with Sections 7159.10 to 7159.14, inclusive. (3) This section does not apply to the sale, installation, and servicing of a fire alarm sold in conjunction with an alarm system, as defined in Section 7590.1, if all costs attributable to making the fire alarm system operable, including sale and installation costs, do not exceed five hundred dollars ($500), and the licensee complies with the requirements set forth in Section 7159.9. (4) This section does not apply to any costs associated with monitoring a burglar or fire alarm system. (5) Failure by the lice
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term to 45 days, potentially conflicting with California's prompt payment laws for construction-related services. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate regulations on payment structures under California Business and Professions Code Section 7159 if the contract falls under home improvement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159, outlines requirements for home improvement contracts, including payment schedules, which the changed term might violate, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will take effect on the Effective Date and remain in effect for a period of 1 year; provided, that Sections 2 and 3 level shall terminate promptly upon (a) any action or omission by Client or any of its customers which constitutes a breach of or default by TrueLink under any System Agreement or Credit Repository Agreement, which breach or default has either not been cured or cannot be cured within the applicable cure period and the consequences of which is that TrueLink will lose material rights it had pursuant to said Agreements or (b) any violations or breach by Client of Sections 8a - 8c.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will take effect on the Effective Date and remain in effect for a period of 1 year; provided, that Sections 2 and 3 level shall terminate immediately upon any action or omission by Client or any of its customers which constitutes a breach of or default by TrueLink under any System Agreement or Credit Repository Agreement or any violations or breach by Client of Sections 8a - 8c.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the cure period for breaches of contract could violate California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act reasonably in exercising their contractual rights. Immediate termination without opportunity to cure may be deemed unreasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code 1203 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement'. Removing the cure period and allowing immediate termination could be interpreted as a violation of good faith, as it doesn't allow the breaching party a chance to rectify the situation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1203. (a) Whether a transaction in the form of a lease creates a lease or security interest is determined by the facts of each case. (b) A transaction in the form of a lease creates a security interest if the consideration that the lessee is to pay the lessor for the right to possession and use of the goods is an obligation for the term of the lease and is not subject to termination by the lessee, and: (1) the original term of the lease is equal to or greater than the remaining economic life of the goods; (2) the lessee is bound to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods or is bound to become the owner of the goods; (3) the lessee has an option to renew the lease for the remaining economic life of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement; or (4) the lessee has an option to become the owner of the goods for no additional consideration or for nominal additional consideration upon compliance with the lease agreement. (c) A transact
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the cure period, which the Perturbation Explanation suggests violates California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation mentions California Commercial Code 1203, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of COM 1203. The LLM generated Law Explanation states that removing the cure period could be interpreted as a violation of good faith, as it doesn't allow the breaching party a chance to rectify the situation. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith because it allows immediate termination without giving the other party a chance to fix the breach.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall renew automatically thereafter for successive one year periods until terminated pursuant to Section 12 herein or unless either Client or TrueLink deliver to the other written notice of intent not to renew no later than thirty (30) days prior to the end of said year.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall renew automatically thereafter for successive one year periods until terminated pursuant to Section 12 herein or unless either Client or TrueLink deliver to the other written notice of intent not to renew no later than fifteen (15) days prior to the end of said year.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notification period to 15 days might be considered unconscionable if it doesn't provide sufficient time for the other party to find an alternative service provider or adjust their business operations. This is especially relevant as this agreement is for licensing and website hosting, which requires a reasonable amount of time to switch services.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 concerns unconscionable contracts. A clause that doesn't provide reasonable notification for non-renewal could be deemed unconscionable, especially if it puts one party at a significant disadvantage. Fifteen days might be insufficient for a business to find a new website hosting or licensing solution, thus rendering the agreement potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification period for non-renewal from 30 days to 15 days. This creates a legal contradiction because, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest, a 15-day notice period may be deemed unconscionable under California Civil Code Section 1670.5, especially for services like website hosting and licensing, where a reasonable amount of time is needed to find alternatives. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that if a court finds a clause of the contract to be unconscionable at the time it was made, the court may refuse to enforce the contract or limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result.
